elements.sent-2411-001: We propose and analyze TRAiL (Tangential Randomization in Linear Bandits), a computationally efficient regret-optimal forced exploration algorithm for linear bandits on action sets that are sublevel sets of strongly convex functions.
elements.sent-2411-002: TRAiL estimates the governing parameter of the linear bandit problem through a standard regularized least squares and perturbs the reward-maximizing action corresponding to said point estimate along the tangent plane of the convex compact action set before projecting back to it.
elements.sent-2411-003: Exploiting concentration results for matrix martingales, we prove that TRAiL ensures a $\Omega(\sqrt{T )$ growth in the inference quality, measured via the minimum eigenvalue of the design (regressor) matrix with high-probability over a $T$-length period.}
elements.sent-2411-004: We build on this result to obtain an $\Ocal(\sqrt{T \log(T))$ upper bound on cumulative regret with probability at least $ 1 - 1/T$ over $T$ periods, and compare TRAiL to other popular algorithms for linear bandits.}

elements.sent-2411-508: Linear bandits define a well-studied class of decision problems, where the stochastic reward is linear in the action that can vary over a continuous set.
elements.sent-2411-509: Opposed to the multi-arm counterpart, this problem offers the ability to garner information about the unknown governing parameter even with sub-optimal ``arms'' or actions, if done correctly.
elements.sent-2411-510: In this paper, we studied a forced exploration algorithm for linear bandits with convex compact action sets.
elements.sent-2411-511: The algorithm adds a noise along the tangent plane of the reward-maximizing action based on the current point estimate of the unknown parameter and then projects it back to the action set.
elements.sent-2411-512: Our analysis guaranteed fast estimation of the unknown parameter with high probability that translated to an $\Ocal(\sqrt{T}\log(T))$ expected regret in high probability.
elements.sent-2411-513: Further analysis of the problem domain revealed that this upper bound was tight up to $\log$ factors.
elements.sent-2411-514: In particular, we provided an $\Omega(\sqrt{T )$ lower bound for linear bandits with a wide variety of action sets and noise processes.}
elements.sent-2411-515: The lower bound analysis also revealed interesting connections between optimal rates for inference and control in linear bandits.
elements.sent-2411-516: We dug deep into examples in the literature where regret-optimal performance was achieved with low inference quality.
elements.sent-2411-517: We provided a geometric argument to establish that such phenomenon is essentially an illusion that is short-lived.
elements.sent-2411-518: The asymptotic regime ties them to the same rate for minimax order-optimal regret.
elements.sent-2411-519: Adaptive control literature on linear bandits is extensive.
elements.sent-2411-520: Perhaps the most well-studied algorithms include UCB and TS.
elements.sent-2411-521: In future work, we want to understand the commonalities among these algorithms and forced exploration with an aim to unify their analyses.
elements.sent-2411-522: A second line of future work includes extension of our work to adaptive control of dynamical systems, such as fully or partially-observed linear time-invariant systems and Markov decision processes.
elements.sent-2411-523: Furthermore, we wish to extend the forced exploration setting and the lower-bound analysis to varying noise processes as in \cite{log_minmax_regret .}
elements.sent-2411-524: Finally, of interest to us, are applications of our methodology to cooperative multi-agent learning contexts in unknown environments.

elements.sent-2411-527: The pseudocodes for LinUCB, TS\footnote{We adjust the factor in $\beta_t'$ with $a_{\max ^2$ to incorporate action sets with $a_{\max \geq 1$.}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{LinUCB Algorithm, adapted from \cite{improved_algorithms_for_stochastic_bandits, context_bandits_ucb}} \label{alg:action_space_sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Data: $\hat{\theta}_1$, T, $\lambda^{UCB}$ \State $V_0\gets\lambda^{UCB} I_n$ \For{$t = 1, \dots, T$} \State $\beta_t \gets M \sqrt{n \log(T (1 + t a_{\max}^2/\lambda^{UCB}))} + \sqrt{\lambda^{UCB}}\theta_{\max}$ \State $a_t\gets\arg \max_{a \in \Acal} \, a^\top \hat{\theta}_t + \beta_t \vnorm{a}_{V_{t-1}^{-1}}$ \State Observe $Y_t$ \State $V_t\gets V_{t-1} + a_t a_t^\top$ \State $\hat{\theta}_{t + 1}\gets V_{t}^{-1} \sum_{s = 1}^t a_s Y_{s}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{TS Algorithm, adapted from \cite{linear_thompson_sampling_revisited}} \label{alg:thompson_sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Data: $\hat{\theta}_1$, T, $\lambda^{TS}$ \State $V_0 \gets \lambda^{TS} I$ \For{$t = 1, \dots, T$} \State $\beta_t' \gets M \sqrt{2 \log(4T^2 (1 + a_{\max}^2 t /\lambda^{TS})^{n/2})} + \sqrt{\lambda^{TS}}\theta_{\max}$ \label{step_beta} \State $\tilde{\theta}_t\gets\hat{\theta}_t + \beta_t' V_{t-1}^{-1/2} \eta_t$, where $\eta_t \sim \Ncal(0, I_n)$ \State $ a_t \gets \arg \max_{a\in \Acal} a^\top \tilde{\theta}_t$ \State Observe $Y_{t}$ \State $V_t\gets V_{t-1} + a_t a_t^\top$ \State $\hat{\theta}_{t+1} \gets V_{t}^{-1} \sum_{s = 1}^{t} a_s Y_{s}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{FEL Algorithm, adapted from \cite{AbbasiYadkori2009ForcedExplorationBA}} \label{alg:fel_algorithm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Data: $\hat{\theta}_1$, T \State $V_0 \gets I$, $f_0 \gets 0$ \State Find an eigenbasis $\{e_i\}_{i = 1}^n$ for $\Delta_g$ with associated eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i\}_{i = 1}^n$. \For{$t = 1, \ldots, T$} \If{$f_{t-1} < c^{FEL} n \sqrt{t}$} \text{: Exploration phase} \State $a_t \gets e_i/\sqrt{\lambda_i}$, where $i \sim \operatorname{Uniform}\{1,...,n\}$ \State Observe $Y_t$ \State $V_t \gets V_{t-1} + a_t a_t^\top$ \State $\hat{\theta}_{t + 1} \gets (V_t)^{-1} \sum_{s = 1}^{t} a_s Y_{s}$ \State $f_t \gets f_{t-1} + 1$ \Else \text{: Exploitation phase} \State $a_t \gets a^\star(\hat{\theta}_t)$ \State Observe $Y_t$ \State Keep previous values: $V_t \gets V_{t-1}$, $\hat{\theta}_{t+1} \gets \hat{\theta}_{t}$, $f_t \gets f_{t-1}$ \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.section-2411-012: High-Probability Guarantees for the FEL Algorithm
